questions.question: Match each neuroimaging method to its primary signal source.
questions.explanation: Each method detects a different physical signal: EEG measures electrical potentials; fMRI detects blood oxygenation changes; PET tracks radioactive tracers; MEG measures magnetic fields; fNIRS uses infrared light to detect blood changes.

questions.question: Match each method to its key strength.
questions.explanation: EEG excels in temporal resolution; fMRI in spatial resolution; TMS provides causal evidence non-invasively; optogenetics offers unparalleled cell-type specificity; DTI reveals structural connectivity via white matter tract mapping.

questions.question: Match each method to its main limitation.
questions.explanation: Every method has key limitations: EEG signals smear through tissue; fMRI is limited by the slow BOLD response; lesions are imprecise with remote effects; TMS only reaches shallow cortex; PET requires radiation exposure.

questions.question: Match each tDCS polarity to its effect.
questions.explanation: Anodal (positive electrode) stimulation depolarises neurons, increasing excitability. Cathodal (negative electrode) stimulation hyperpolarises neurons, decreasing excitability. This bidirectional control is a key feature of tDCS.

questions.question: Match each fMRI design type to its description.
categories: Correlations between brain regions over time

correct_mapping.Functional connectivity: Correlations between brain regions over time

questions.explanation: Block designs compare extended periods of different tasks; event-related designs isolate responses to individual events; MVPA decodes information from multi-voxel patterns; functional connectivity examines temporal correlations between regions.

questions.question: Match each BOLD response timing to its description.
questions.explanation: The hemodynamic response function follows a predictable time course: onset at 1-2 sec after neural activity, peak amplitude at 5-6 sec, and return to baseline at approximately 12-15 sec.

questions.question: Match each method category to its examples.
questions.explanation: Perturbation methods disrupt function (lesions, TMS, tDCS, optogenetics); neuromonitoring observes activity (EEG, fMRI, PET, MEG); connectivity methods analyse relationships between regions (PPI, DCM, SEM); DTI maps structural white matter connections.

questions.question: Match each type of evidence to its strength of inference.
categories: Damage to A impairs X but not Y
A impairs X not Y AND B impairs Y not X (strongest for independence)
Multiple methods agree (strongest for overall conclusion)

correct_mapping.Single dissociation: Damage to A impairs X but not Y
correct_mapping.Double dissociation: A impairs X not Y AND B impairs Y not X (strongest for independence)
correct_mapping.Convergence across methods: Multiple methods agree (strongest for overall conclusion)

questions.explanation: Inferential strength increases from simple associations (just activation) through single dissociations to double dissociations (strongest evidence for functional independence). Convergence across methods strengthens any individual finding.

questions.question: Match each method to whether it provides causal or correlational evidence.
questions.explanation: Perturbation methods (lesions, TMS, optogenetics) provide causal evidence by disrupting function and observing consequences. Neuromonitoring methods (fMRI, EEG, MEG) observe activity during tasks and provide correlational evidence.

questions.question: Match each method to its approximate spatial and temporal resolution category.
questions.explanation: EEG has millisecond temporal but poor spatial resolution; fMRI has millimetre spatial but seconds-level temporal resolution; PET has poor resolution on both dimensions; MEG has millisecond temporal and somewhat better spatial resolution than EEG (magnetic fields not distorted by tissue).
